editableNewDialogue.text: Can you explain the difference between a virus and a bacterium in simple words?

A bacterium is a living, single-celled organism that can often grow and reproduce on its own. A virus is not really “alive” in the same way; it’s more like a tiny package of genetic material that must enter a living cell to make more copies of itself. Many bacterial infections can be treated with antibiotics, but antibiotics do not work on viruses.
Example of a bacterium: Streptococcus (some types can cause strep throat). Example of a virus: influenza virus (causes the flu).
Random question: why do some movies use a Wilhelm scream?

It’s a well-known stock sound effect originally recorded for older films. Directors and sound editors reuse it as an inside joke and a small tribute to classic cinema—kind of like a hidden “Easter egg” for people who recognize it.
Give me three quick, interesting facts.

1) Honey can last a very long time without spoiling because it’s low in water and naturally antimicrobial.
2) Octopuses have three hearts and blue blood due to a copper-based oxygen carrier.
3) The Voynich manuscript is a famous illustrated book with writing that still isn’t conclusively deciphered.
I'm trying to focus on the bone stuff again, but English isn’t my first language, so I keep mixing up the medical words—can you explain it in really simple terms?
Absolutely. I can explain bone cancer in very simple language and define any words as we go. Tell me what part is most confusing right now—symptoms, types, tests, or treatments?
About my dog being buried—I've been mixing things up. Miguel Madriz isn't in Venezuela, and honestly he doesn't know where my dog was buried either. I mentioned him by mistake.

Thanks for clarifying. So Miguel Madriz is not in Venezuela, and he also doesn't have the burial address information. If you want, tell me what you *do* remember (approximate area, date, who was with you), and I can help you think of ways to track it down.
